pin_title: What 'Good Source of Fiber' Actually Means on a Label
pin_description: That front-of-box claim has a legal definition, and it's smaller than you think. See what 'good source of fiber' really guarantees before you pay extra. Get the breakdown.
alt_text: A nutrition facts label on a counter beside a magnifying glass and a bowl of cereal.

pin_title: The 3-Gram Rule Hiding Behind Fiber Label Claims
pin_description: 'Good source' can mean as little as 3 grams per serving. Here's the FDA math behind front-of-box fiber claims, in plain English. Click for the label cheat sheet.
alt_text: Cereal boxes on a grocery shelf with one turned to show the nutrition panel.

pin_title: Stop Falling for the Front of the Box. Read This Instead
pin_description: The front of the package is marketing. The nutrition panel is the truth. Learn the two numbers that tell you whether a fiber claim is real. See the full guide.
alt_text: The nutrition panel of a granola package on a table beside a pen and notepad.

pin_title: High Fiber vs Good Source of Fiber: Not the Same Thing
pin_description: One claim means 20 percent of your daily fiber, the other can be half that. The difference is printed on the label once you know where to look. Get the comparison.
alt_text: Two cereal boxes side by side on a counter with a measuring cup of bran cereal.
